perturbation.original_text: “Target Confidential Information ” means Information falling within paragraphs (a) to (c) of the definition of “ Confidential Information ”.
perturbation.changed_text: “Target Confidential Information ” means Information falling within paragraphs (a) to (b) of the definition of “ Confidential Information ”.
perturbation.explanation: By removing (c) from the definition of Target Confidential Information in Section 1.1, Section 2.3 which states 'Subject always to paragraph 4.3, we shall treat and keep all Target Confidential Information as confidential and shall not, without your prior written consent, directly or indirectly disclose such Target Confidential Information to any other person other than (i) as permitted by par agraph 3.1 , (ii) to Jardine Matheson Holdings Limited (or any member of its group or its advisers in connection with the Possible Transaction) and (iii) to the Takeover Panel and the Financial Conduct Authority', now faces an in-text contradiction regarding the confidentiality of discussions and negotiations. Since (c) relates to the existence and content of discussions and negotiations between parties, this information is no longer automatically protected as Target Confidential Information. This omission creates ambiguity around whether these specific types of information are subject to the same confidentiality constraints, because one section says is confidential and the other one says is not, thus generating a contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Subject always to paragraph 4.3, we shall treat and keep all Target Confidential Information as confidential and shall not, without your prior written consent, directly or indirectly disclose such Target Confidential Information to any other person other than (i) as permitted by par agraph 3.1 , (ii) to Jardine Matheson Holdings Limited (or any member of its group or its advisers in connection with the Possible Transaction) and (iii) to the Takeover Panel and the Financial Conduct Authority .
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the definition of "Target Confidential Information" by removing paragraph (c). According to the perturbation explanation, paragraph (c) relates to the existence and content of discussions and negotiations between parties. The contradicted text states that "Target Confidential Information" shall be kept confidential. By removing (c) from the definition of "Target Confidential Information", the changed text creates a contradiction because it implies that the discussions and negotiations are no longer automatically protected as confidential, directly conflicting with the contradicted text which requires all "Target Confidential Information" to be kept confidential.

perturbation.original_text: save that you will be entitled to retain such copies of such Confidential Information (a) to the extent required by law or regulation , (b) to the extent required by your internal compliance procedures , or (c) to the extent contained as back -ups or archives as a matter of reasonable routine process on your electronic information management and communications systems or servers and for which deletion or destruction would not be reasonably prac ticable (provided that no step will be taken to access or recover such Confidential Information and provided that such Confidential Information shall continue to be held subject to the terms of this agreement) .
perturbation.changed_text: save that you will be entitled to retain such copies of such Confidential Information (a) to the extent required by law or regulation , (b) to the extent required by your internal compliance procedures .
perturbation.explanation: By omitting clause '(c) to the extent contained as back -ups or archives as a matter of reasonable routine process on your electronic information management and communications systems or servers and for which deletion or destruction would not be reasonably prac ticable (provided that no step will be taken to access or recover such Confidential Information and provided that such Confidential Information shall continue to be held subject to the terms of this agreement)' from Section 5.1.2, it implies that retention of confidential information in backups and archives is no longer permitted, but the clause about the confidential information being held subject to the terms of this agreement after being retained, still remains. This now contradicts the following Section 5.2, which states 'Any Confidential Information which, notwithstanding paragraph 5.1 is retained, will continue to be held subject to the terms of this agreement and you shall not further use or disclos e to any person any such Confidential Information.' If Section 5.1 now prohibits the retention of information in backups, it raises a contradiction because Section 5.2 still anticipates the possibility of retained confidential information being held subject to the agreement, leading to ambiguity around the permissible retention of backups.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Any Confidential Information which, notwithstanding paragraph 5.1 is retained, will continue to be held subject to the terms of this agreement and you shall not further use or disclos e to any person any such Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The original text in Section 5.1.2 allowed for the retention of confidential information in backups under specific conditions. The changed text omits this allowance. Section 5.2 explicitly refers to 'Any Confidential Information which, notwithstanding paragraph 5.1 is retained'. By removing the clause allowing retention in backups from Section 5.1.2, the changed text implies that such retention is no longer permitted, which contradicts Section 5.2's assumption that some confidential information might still be retained, leading to an inconsistency. This creates a strong in-text contradiction, as defined by 'Omissions - In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: 6.3.2 solicit, engage or employ (whether paid or unpaid) any Restricted Person of the other Party . For the avoidance of doubt this restriction shall not prohibit either Party from engaging or employing any such Restricted Person who has responded to a bona fide recruitment advertisement not specifically targeted at such Restricted P erson or from engaging or employing any Restricted Person whose employment has been terminated by the party by whom such Restricted Person was employed, provided that such employment and engagement commences following such termination.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.3.2 solicit, engage or employ (whether paid or unpaid) any Restricted Person of the other Party .
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clarification 'For the avoidance of doubt this restriction shall not prohibit either Party from engaging or employing any such Restricted Person who has responded to a bona fide recruitment advertisement not specifically targeted at such Restricted P erson or from engaging or employing any Restricted Person whose employment has been terminated by the party by whom such Restricted Person was employed, provided that such employment and engagement commences following such termination.' from section 6.3.2, it contradicts the implications of Section 6.3.1, which discusses engaging in discussions with employees, including restricted persons. The absence of these clarifications in 6.3.2 creates an in-text contradiction because the employment restrictions on restricted persons become stricter, conflicting with the implication that some engagement with them is permissible under 6.3.1. This generates uncertainty about the intended scope of employment restrictions concerning restricted persons.
perturbation.contradicted_text: except as otherwise permitted by this Agreement and subject to paragraph 6.4, initiate or knowingly engage in discussion s in connection with the Possible Transaction with any shareholder , employee (including the JLT directors nominated by Jardine Matheson Holdings L imited (or its subsidiary) but excluding any other director of a Party who is also an employee) , customer or supplier of or lender to any member of the other party 's Group ; or
perturbation.justification: The original text of section 6.3.2 allowed for the engagement or employment of restricted persons who responded to a bona fide recruitment advertisement or whose employment had been terminated by the other party. This is consistent with section 6.3.1, which allows for discussions with employees. The changed text of section 6.3.2 removes this allowance, creating a contradiction with the implications of section 6.3.1, which allows for discussions, because the employment restrictions on restricted persons becomes stricter in 6.3.2. The absence of these clarifications creates an in-text contradiction because it implies that any engagement with restricted persons is now prohibited, conflicting with the implication that some engagement with them is permissible under 6.3.1.

perturbation.original_text: 13. THIRD PARTY RIGHTS The provisions of this agreement confer benefits on the persons referred to in paragraph 1.4 (other than us) (each, a " Third Party ") and each Third Party will have the right under the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999 (the " 1999 Act ") to enforce its respective rights under this agreement. The parties to this agreement do not require the consent of any Third Party to rescind or vary this ag reement (other than paragraphs 10, 13 and 16) at any time. No other person who is not a party to this agreement has any right under the 1999 Act to enforce any term of this agreement but this does not affect any right or remedy of a third party which exists or is available apart from the 1999 Act.
perturbation.changed_text: 13. THIRD PARTY RIGHTS The provisions of this agreement confer benefits on the persons referred to in paragraph 1.4 (other than us) (each, a " Third Party ") and each Third Party will have the right under the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999 (the " 1999 Act ") to enforce its respective rights under this agreement. The parties to this agreement do not require the consent of any Third Party to rescind or vary this ag reement (other than paragraphs 10 and 16) at any time. No other person who is not a party to this agreement has any right under the 1999 Act to enforce any term of this agreement but this does not affect any right or remedy of a third party which exists or is available apart from the 1999 Act.
perturbation.explanation: By removing '13' from the exclusions (other than paragraphs 10, 13 and 16) in section 13, which previously required third-party consent to alter section 13 itself, now section 13 could be altered without the need of the third-party consent. However, this contradicts itself in the following sentence, because 'No other person who is not a party to this agreement has any right under the 1999 Act to enforce any term of this agreement but this does not affect any right or remedy of a third party which exists or is available apart from the 1999 Act', implying that the third party still holds rights which exist or are available apart from the 1999 Act, thus creating an in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 13. THIRD PARTY RIGHTS The provisions of this agreement confer benefits on the persons referred to in paragraph 1.4 (other than us) (each, a " Third Party ") and each Third Party will have the right under the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999 (the " 1999 Act ") to enforce its respective rights under this agreement. The parties to this agreement do not require the consent of any Third Party to rescind or vary this ag reement (other than paragraphs 10 and 16) at any time. No other person who is not a party to this agreement has any right under the 1999 Act to enforce any term of this agreement but this does not affect any right or remedy of a third party which exists or is available apart from the 1999 Act.
perturbation.justification: The original text states that the parties do not require the consent of any Third Party to rescind or vary this agreement (other than paragraphs 10, 13 and 16) at any time. The changed text removes '13' from the exclusions, meaning that section 13 could now be altered without the third-party consent. This change directly contradicts the following sentence which implies that the third party still holds rights which exist or are available apart from the 1999 Act. The removal of '13' from the exclusion list directly impacts the third party's right, thus creating a strong in-text contradiction based on the omission.
